title: Life is Strange Remastered
summary: A classic for a reason. Interesting story and good characters, with a super power twist.
body: If you have a choice just buy the original. Graphics are really a side grade at best and bugs were added that the original doesn't have.

tabs.name: The Good, The Bad, & The Okay
tabs.content:
[h1]The Good[/h1][list]
[*] Great Story
[*] Complex Characters with thought out backgrounds
[*] Unique Hand Crafted Menus and interface prompts
[*] Community Choice Comparisons
[*]Memorable Music
[/list]
[h1]The Bad[/h1][list]
[*]Episodic Releases
[/list]
[h1]The Okay[/h1][list][*] Choices Barely Matter, generally resulted in dialogue changes but not actual story changes. While the character's feelings towards Max were altered the overarching story remained the same except for 2 choices.
[/list]

tabs.name: Game Mechanics
tabs.content:
[tab=Time Rewind]
[h1]What Is The Mechanic[/h1]Player keeps inventory and position on map but the local events are rewound

[h1]How I Think This Works[/h1]My first thoughts were that everything would be tracked and therefore you just rewind literally everything on the screen however much time has passed.

Thinking about it more this is clearly not the case since the amount of time doesn't matter. In fact the features of Max's rewind power kind of confirm all this since the game doesn't reset your position or inventory. 

So instead of rewinding the timeline I believe that each map has local events to trigger and after you trigger them it is added to the rewind bar, so when  you rewind it references that event and resets it once you rewind past it.

Upon further review it looks like a combination. When you reach an event it saves a checkpoint of sorts, then the rewind bar rewinds up to the max duration of that event. Most characters on the scene are on loop but one event runs for a duration before hitting an end point that is either off screen or a loop once again. When you use rewind it rewinds from the end point up to the start point. 
[/tab]
[tab=Point & Click]
[h1]What Is The Mechanic[/h1]When you get close to an interactable object text appears on the screen and a button can be pressed to interact with it.

[h1]How I Think This Works[/h1]Interacable objects have two boundaries around them that do things when a character crosses the edge.

The outer boundary makes text appear on the screen that serves as a label for the object. The inner boundary brings up the interacting ui so you can do something with that object, whether that be picking up, moving, or just getting information about.
[/tab]
